stem: A superficial spreading melanoma is sent for molecular testing. Mutation of which pathway is present (BRAF and NRAS combined) in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas AND benign melanocytic nevi, marking it as the central driver of melanocytic neoplasia?

options.A: BRAF or NRAS (MAPK pathway)
options.B: GNAQ or GNA11 (Gq pathway)
options.C: KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.D: PTEN loss (PI3K–AKT pathway)

exp: Melanoma is fundamentally a disease of constitutive MAPK (RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK) signaling: a BRAF or NRAS mutation is present in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas and also in ordinary melanocytic nevi, which is why MAPK activation alone causes only nevus formation/senescence until secondary hits permit malignant progression. The other options are real drivers in *different* melanocytic compartments — GNAQ/GNA11 define uveal melanoma and blue nevi (Gq→PKC signaling), KIT typifies acral/mucosal/high-CSD melanoma, and PTEN loss augments PI3K–AKT signaling as a cooperating (not initiating) event.
hy: BRAF/NRAS (MAPK) is mutated in up to 80% of cutaneous melanomas and nevi — the core driver axis.

stem: BRAF V600E, the commonest BRAF mutation in melanoma, drives constitutive MAPK signaling through which precise molecular change?

options.A: Substitution of glutamic acid for valine at codon 600
options.B: Substitution of lysine for valine at codon 600
options.C: An in-frame kinase fusion that deletes the autoregulatory domain
options.D: A promoter mutation that creates a new transcription-factor binding site

exp: V600E denotes substitution of glutamic acid (E) for valine (V) at codon 600, locking BRAF kinase "on" and driving the MAPK cascade independent of upstream receptor input; it is the basis for BRAF-inhibitor therapy. The distractors are each a genuine alternative mechanism: V600K (lysine for valine) is a real non-E variant enriched in high-CSD melanoma; kinase fusions that excise the regulatory domain activate "pan-negative" melanomas; and a promoter mutation creating a new binding site is the TERT-promoter mechanism, a separate non-coding lesion.
hy: V600E = glutamic acid→valine at codon 600 → constitutive MAPK; V600K is the high-CSD variant.

stem: A melanoma on the sole and a separate sinonasal mucosal melanoma are tested. Mutation/amplification of which gene is most characteristic of acral lentiginous and mucosal melanomas, raising the possibility of a KIT-inhibitor (e.g. imatinib)?

options.A: KIT
options.B: BRAF V600E
options.C: CDKN2A
options.D: GNA11

exp: KIT activation (point mutation or amplification) is found in ~15–40% of acral lentiginous, mucosal, and high-CSD melanomas, and because KIT is a receptor tyrosine kinase these tumors can respond to KIT inhibitors such as imatinib (though response rates are limited). The distractors anchor to other sites: BRAF V600E dominates non-CSD/SSM melanoma, CDKN2A is the familial-melanoma germline gene (~2% of sporadic tumors), and GNA11 marks uveal melanoma.
hy: KIT = acral/mucosal/high-CSD melanoma → potential imatinib target.

stem: Which statement about GNAQ/GNA11 mutations is correct?

options.A: They occur in 80–90% of uveal melanomas and in blue nevi, yet are absent in cutaneous melanoma
options.B: They are the single most common driver found in high-CSD cutaneous melanomas of the elderly
options.C: They are present in roughly 50–60% of superficial spreading (low-CSD) cutaneous melanomas
options.D: They are the activating mutation that defines Spitz melanomas and atypical Spitz tumors

exp: GNAQ and GNA11 encode Gq-class G-protein α-subunits; their activating mutations occur in 80–90% of uveal melanomas and in blue nevi, but characteristically NOT in conventional cutaneous melanoma — a classic discriminator. The wrong options misassign drivers: high-CSD cutaneous melanoma is most often NRAS-mutant, SSM is typically BRAF V600E, and Spitz tumors carry HRAS mutations or kinase fusions.
hy: GNAQ/GNA11 = uveal melanoma + blue nevi, not cutaneous melanoma.

stem: CDKN2A is the major high-penetrance familial-melanoma gene. Which statement about it is correct?

options.A: It encodes both p16 and p14ARF, and explains roughly 2% of all cutaneous melanomas
options.B: It encodes the BRAF kinase and is somatically mutated in about half of all melanomas
options.C: Its germline loss is the defining lesion underlying the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome
options.D: It is amplified rather than deleted in the large majority of uveal (choroidal) melanomas

exp: The CDKN2A locus uniquely encodes two proteins via alternate reading frames — p16 (inhibits CDK4/6 → Rb pathway) and p14ARF (inhibits MDM2 → stabilizes p53) — so its loss disables two checkpoints; it is the most common germline mutation in familial melanoma yet explains only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas. The distractors describe other genes: BRAF is the kinase mutated in ~50% of melanomas, BAP1 (not CDKN2A) defines the BAP1 tumor syndrome, and uveal melanomas are driven by GNAQ/GNA11 with BAP1 loss, not CDKN2A amplification.
hy: CDKN2A → p16 + p14ARF; commonest familial gene but ~2% of sporadic melanoma.

stem: Loss of NF1, activating both the MAPK and PI3K pathways, is most characteristically found in which melanoma context?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma (~25%) and high-CSD melanoma (~30%)
options.B: Uveal melanoma (~50%) and melanoma arising in blue nevi
options.C: Acral lentiginous and mucosal melanoma (~15–40%)
options.D: Spitz melanoma and atypical Spitz tumors of childhood

exp: NF1 is a RAS-GAP tumor suppressor; its loss releases RAS, activating both MAPK and PI3K, and is found in ~25% of desmoplastic melanomas and ~30% of high-CSD melanomas. The distractors place the wrong driver at each site: uveal melanoma is GNAQ/GNA11 (with BAP1 loss in ~50%), acral melanoma favors KIT/NRAS, and Spitz tumors carry HRAS or fusions.
hy: NF1 loss = desmoplastic (~25%) and high-CSD (~30%) melanoma.

stem: A germline E318K variant in MITF is associated with which phenotype?

options.A: Light skin, high nevus count, and increased melanoma and renal cell carcinoma risk
options.B: Café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, neurofibromas, and optic glioma risk
options.C: Numerous early basal cell carcinomas with odontogenic jaw cysts and palmar pits
options.D: Marked photosensitivity and early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair

exp: The MITF p.E318K germline variant impairs SUMOylation of this master melanocyte transcription factor, producing a light-skin/high-nevus-count phenotype with increased risk of melanoma (including amelanotic) and renal cell carcinoma. Each distractor is a different cancer-predisposition phenotype: café-au-lait macules with axillary freckling and neurofibromas is NF1; numerous early BCCs with jaw cysts is Gorlin (nevoid BCC) syndrome; and photosensitivity with early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair is xeroderma pigmentosum.
hy: MITF E318K = light skin, ↑nevi, melanoma + RCC risk.

stem: A "pan-negative" melanoma (wild-type BRAF, NRAS, NF1) is found to carry an NTRK gene fusion. Which agent specifically targets this alteration?

options.A: Larotrectinib, a selective TRK inhibitor
options.B: Vemurafenib, a selective BRAF V600 inhibitor
options.C: Imatinib, a KIT/ABL receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor
options.D: Trametinib, an allosteric downstream MEK pathway inhibitor

exp: A subset of BRAF/NRAS/NF1-negative ("pan-negative") melanomas harbor MAPK-activating kinase fusions, including NTRK fusions, which are actionable with the selective TRK inhibitor larotrectinib. The distractors require a different lesion: vemurafenib needs a BRAF V600 mutation, imatinib targets KIT (acral/mucosal melanoma), and trametinib is a MEK inhibitor used with a BRAF inhibitor, not a stand-alone match for an NTRK fusion.
hy: NTRK fusion melanoma → larotrectinib (TRK inhibitor).

stem: The 2018/2023 WHO classification of melanoma is organized primarily around which axis?

options.A: Cumulative sun damage (high-, low-, non-CSD) plus site of origin
options.B: The Breslow thickness category as measured at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The presence or absence of histologic ulceration of the primary tumor
options.D: Predominance of the radial versus the vertical growth phase histologically

exp: The WHO taxonomy of melanocytic neoplasia is built on the degree of cumulative sun damage (high-, low-, or non-CSD) integrated with site of origin and the frequency of BRAF/NRAS and other mutations — linking biology to anatomy. The distractors are all genuine prognostic/morphologic variables (Breslow thickness and ulceration drive AJCC T-stage; radial vs vertical growth phase is a histologic concept), but none is the organizing axis of the WHO classification.
hy: WHO melanoma classes = cumulative sun damage + site + mutation pattern.

stem: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A belong to which class of melanoma antigen?

options.A: Melanocyte differentiation (lineage) antigens
options.B: Cancer/testis shared tumor-specific antigens
options.C: Mutant tumor-specific neoantigens (e.g. p16)
options.D: Viral oncoproteins from an oncogenic virus

exp: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A are melanocyte *differentiation* (lineage) antigens — normal melanosomal proteins overexpressed by melanoma and recognized by autologous T cells. The distractors are the other antigen categories the chapter lists: cancer/testis antigens are MAGE-1/-3 and NY-ESO-1; mutant neoantigens include altered p16 (CDKN2A); and viral oncoproteins drive a different skin tumor (Merkel-cell carcinoma), not conventional melanoma.
hy: Tyrosinase/gp100/MART-1 = melanocyte differentiation antigens.

stem: CTLA-4, the target of ipilimumab, inhibits T-cell activation by which mechanism?

options.A: An inhibitory CD28 homolog that competes for B7, removing costimulation
options.B: A receptor whose ligand PD-L1 triggers apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells
options.C: An inhibitory receptor blocked by the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab
options.D: A molecule presenting antigen on MHC class I to cytotoxic T cells

exp: CTLA-4 is an inhibitory homolog of CD28 that is upregulated after T-cell activation and outcompetes CD28 for B7 on antigen-presenting cells, removing the costimulatory signal and acting as a natural "brake"; ipilimumab blocks it. The distractors describe sibling checkpoints/mechanisms: the PD-1/PD-L1 axis induces T-cell apoptosis at the effector phase, LAG3 is the relatlimab target, and antigen presentation on MHC I is the function of the tumor/APC, not CTLA-4.
hy: CTLA-4 = CD28 homolog that steals B7 → removes costimulation (ipilimumab target).

stem: During checkpoint-inhibitor therapy, development of vitiligo-like leukoderma is notable because it:

options.A: Correlates with improved progression-free survival
options.B: Mandates immediate permanent discontinuation of immunotherapy
options.C: Signals progression to distant metastatic disease
options.D: Predicts treatment failure and a switch to chemotherapy

exp: Vitiligo-like leukoderma is a melanocyte-directed immune-related adverse event occurring in ~2–25% of patients, and its appearance correlates with improved progression-free survival — a favorable on-treatment sign rather than a reason to stop therapy. The distractors invert this: it does not require discontinuation, does not signal metastatic progression, and does not predict failure.
hy: Checkpoint-inhibitor vitiligo = good prognostic sign (better PFS).

stem: Which is a recognized mechanism of melanoma immune escape?

options.A: Loss of MHC class I molecules from the tumor-cell surface
options.B: Upregulation of MHC class II on all surrounding keratinocytes
options.C: Increased CD28-mediated costimulation of cytotoxic T cells
options.D: Enhanced presentation of tumor neoantigens to dendritic cells

exp: Tumors evade immunity by losing antigen-presentation machinery — including loss of MHC class I — alongside loss of tumor antigens, secretion of inhibitory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), and upregulation of inhibitory ligands (PD-L1, CTLA-4). The distractors are all immune-*promoting* (more MHC II, more CD28 costimulation, more neoantigen presentation), the opposite of escape.
hy: Immune escape = lose MHC I/antigens, secrete IL-10/TGF-β, upregulate PD-L1/CTLA-4.

stem: Immune checkpoint blockade was first introduced clinically for melanoma. Which agent was approved first?

options.A: Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4)
options.B: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1)
options.C: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
options.D: Relatlimab (anti-LAG3)

exp: Ipilimumab, an anti-CTLA-4 antibody, was the first checkpoint inhibitor approved and was introduced first for melanoma; anti-PD-1 antibodies (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) followed as first-line monotherapy, and the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab is the most recent (with nivolumab). The distractors are all real checkpoint agents but later to approval.
hy: Checkpoint blockade debuted in melanoma with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4).

stem: The PD-1/PD-L1 interaction promotes tumor immune escape by:

options.A: Driving apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells while sparing regulatory T cells
options.B: Competing with CD28 for B7 to strip away the early costimulatory signal
options.C: Blocking the loading of antigenic peptides onto tumor-cell MHC class I
options.D: Preventing antigen-loaded dendritic cells from migrating to the draining node

exp: PD-1 engagement by PD-L1/PD-L2 drives apoptosis of tumor-antigen-specific effector T cells while reducing apoptosis of regulatory T cells, tilting the microenvironment toward tolerance at the effector phase. The distractors belong elsewhere: competing with CD28 for B7 is the CTLA-4 mechanism, and blocking MHC-I loading or dendritic-cell migration to nodes are different (afferent-phase) escape routes.
hy: PD-1/PD-L1 = apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells, sparing Tregs (effector-phase brake).

stem: The annual increase in cutaneous melanoma incidence in White populations has generally been estimated at:

options.A: 3–7% per year, predicting a doubling every 10–20 years
options.B: Under 1% per year, with essentially stable long-term rates
options.C: About 15% per year, the fastest rate of any human cancer
options.D: About 25% per year, doubling roughly every 3 years

exp: Across populations the annual rise in melanoma incidence is roughly 3–7%, which predicts a doubling every 10–20 years and makes melanoma the most rapidly increasing cancer in White populations (partly genuine, partly enhanced detection). The distractors are the neighbouring wrong magnitudes: stable/<1% understates it, and 15–25%/year with 3-year doubling wildly overstates it.
hy: Melanoma incidence rising ~3–7%/yr → doubling every 10–20 yr.

stem: Which region carries the world's highest cutaneous melanoma incidence rates?

options.A: Australia/New Zealand (~54 per 100,000 per year)
options.B: Northern Europe (~25–26 per 100,000 per year)
options.C: North America (high but below Australasia)
options.D: Southern Europe (lower than Northern Europe)

exp: Australia and New Zealand carry the world's highest melanoma incidence (~54/100,000), driven by fair-skinned populations under intense UV; Northern Europe is the next-highest band (~25/100,000). The other regions (North America, Southern Europe) have substantial but lower rates, so the discriminator is recalling that Australia/NZ leads.
hy: Highest melanoma incidence = Australia/New Zealand (~54/100,000).

stem: Which statement about melanoma mortality and tumor-thickness trends is correct?

options.A: Mortality has stabilized/declined since the 1990s, though older men still show rising rates
options.B: Mortality has fallen uniformly across every age group and both sexes in all countries worldwide
options.C: Mean Breslow thickness at diagnosis has risen steadily across recent decades in most populations
options.D: The proportion of thick melanomas at diagnosis falls progressively with each increase in patient age

exp: Melanoma mortality has broadly stabilized or declined since the 1990s — chiefly from earlier detection of thinner, curable tumors and, more recently, effective systemic therapy — even as incidence keeps rising; the exception is a continued rise among older men. The distractors are real-sounding but false: the decline is *not* uniform (older men rise), mean Breslow thickness has *fallen* with earlier detection, and the proportion of thick melanomas *increases* with age (≈20% by age 80).
hy: Mortality stabilizing (thinner tumors), but older men still rising; thick-tumor share ↑ with age.

stem: A patient has more than 100 common melanocytic nevi. Approximately what relative risk of melanoma does this confer?

options.A: ~8- to 10-fold
options.B: ~2-fold
options.C: ~3- to 4-fold
options.D: ~20- to 30-fold

exp: Having >100 (or even >50) common melanocytic nevi confers a relative melanoma risk of roughly 8–10-fold — nevus count is one of the strongest phenotypic risk markers because it reflects both UV exposure and a melanocytic-proliferation phenotype. The distractors are the adjacent risk magnitudes for *other* lesion categories (solar lentigines ~3–4×; atypical-nevus thresholds up to ~30×), so the reader must match the right lesion to the right number.
hy: >100 common nevi ≈ RR 8–10×; the dose-response is near-linear.

stem: Above how many clinically atypical melanocytic nevi does relative risk rise sharply (defining atypical mole syndrome), with little further increase beyond that threshold?

options.A: 5 or more
options.B: 2 or more
options.C: 10 or more
options.D: 50 or more

exp: Five or more clinically atypical nevi marks a sharp step-up in risk and is the threshold used to define atypical mole syndrome (typically alongside ≥50, often >100, common nevi); beyond five there is little *further* incremental risk in the studies. The distractors are the adjacent counts that don't define the threshold (2 is too few; 10 is where the maximum ~32× risk is reported; 50 refers to common-nevus count).
hy: ≥5 atypical nevi = atypical mole syndrome threshold; risk plateaus beyond.

stem: Which phenotype reflects the pheomelanin-predominant, high-risk pigmentation pattern?

options.A: Red hair and blue eyes, a tendency to burn, and an inability to tan
options.B: Black hair and brown eyes that tan readily without ever burning
options.C: Extensive congenital dermal melanocytosis over the trunk and buttocks
options.D: Constitutively dark (Fitzpatrick phototype V–VI) sun-resistant skin

exp: Red hair with blue eyes and a burn-without-tan phenotype reflects pheomelanin-predominant pigmentation (often MC1R-related), a well-established melanoma risk phenotype because pheomelanin offers little UV protection and may itself be pro-oxidant. The distractors are lower-risk patterns: a readily-tanning dark-haired/brown-eyed phenotype, dermal melanocytosis (benign), and constitutively dark skin all carry lower melanoma risk.
hy: Red hair/blue eyes/burns-not-tans (pheomelanin) = high-risk phenotype.

stem: Regarding ultraviolet exposure and melanoma, which statement is correct?

options.A: Indoor tanning before age 35 substantially raises risk and is a classified human carcinogen
options.B: Intense intermittent sun exposure lowers melanoma risk relative to chronic daily exposure
options.C: Blistering childhood sunburns bear essentially no relationship to later melanoma risk
options.D: Residence at equatorial latitudes is protective through gradual pigment adaptation

exp: Indoor tanning, especially before age 35, substantially raises melanoma risk and is classified by the WHO/IARC as a human carcinogen — the basis for legislation restricting minors' access. The distractors are false reversals: intense *intermittent* sun (and blistering childhood sunburns) *raises* risk, sunburns are firmly linked to melanoma, and equatorial residence increases (not lowers) risk.
hy: Tanning beds <35 = carcinogen, ↑melanoma; intermittent sun & childhood sunburns also raise risk.

stem: Which population has increased melanoma risk specifically because of acquired immunosuppression?

options.A: Solid-organ transplant recipients and people living with HIV infection
options.B: Patients using topical retinoids long-term for chronic inflammatory acne
options.C: Patients with active, spreading, non-segmental generalized vitiligo
options.D: Otherwise healthy women taking combined oral contraceptive pills

exp: Acquired immunosuppression — solid-organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressants and people with HIV — carries increased melanoma risk (reduced immune surveillance). The distractors are non-risks or even inverse: topical retinoids are not a melanoma risk, vitiligo correlates with *better* checkpoint-therapy response, and oral contraceptives have not been shown to increase melanoma risk.
hy: Transplant recipients and HIV = immunosuppression-related ↑melanoma risk.

stem: Why is prophylactic removal of clinically banal melanocytic nevi NOT recommended?

options.A: Single-nevus transformation is ~1/thousands (common) to 1/hundreds (atypical), and most melanoma is de novo
options.B: Banal melanocytic nevi essentially never carry any of the driver mutations that are also seen in melanoma
options.C: Excising a melanocytic nevus paradoxically raises the risk of a melanoma developing later at distant sites
options.D: Melanomas that arise within a pre-existing benign nevus are uniformly fatal regardless of complete excision

exp: Because the malignant-transformation risk of any single nevus is tiny — on the order of 1 in thousands for a common nevus and 1 in hundreds for an atypical one — and most melanomas arise de novo rather than within a nevus, removing banal nevi prophylactically is not justified. The distractors are false: nevi *can* harbor BRAF and other melanoma drivers, excision does not raise distant risk, and nevus-associated melanomas are not uniformly fatal.
hy: Single-nevus transformation ≈ 1/thousands–1/hundreds; most melanoma is de novo → don't excise banal nevi.

stem: Approximately what proportion of familial melanoma kindreds carry a germline CDKN2A mutation?

options.B: About 2%
options.D: About 5%

exp: Germline CDKN2A mutations are detected in 20–40% of familial melanoma kindreds (range 5–70% by region). The key trap is the adjacent figure ~2%, which is the fraction of *all cutaneous melanomas* attributable to CDKN2A — a different denominator. The other values (70–90%, ~5%) are simply wrong magnitudes for kindred penetrance.
hy: CDKN2A in 20–40% of familial kindreds, but only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas.

stem: Which statement about MC1R variants and melanoma is correct?

options.A: They are low/moderate-penetrance pigment alleles; two variants raise BRAF-mutant melanoma risk up to ~17×
options.B: They constitute the single highest-penetrance familial melanoma susceptibility gene currently known
options.C: They encode the p16 tumor-suppressor protein that brakes the CDK4/6–Rb cell-cycle checkpoint
options.D: They are somatic UV-induced mutations rather than inherited germline pigmentation gene variants

exp: MC1R variants are common, low-to-moderate-penetrance pigmentation alleles; while they raise overall melanoma risk only ~1.5–3×, carrying two variants raises the risk of *BRAF-mutant* melanoma up to ~17-fold — a striking gene-specific interaction. The distractors misclass MC1R: the highest-penetrance familial gene is CDKN2A, p16 is encoded by CDKN2A (not MC1R), and MC1R variants are germline, not somatic.
hy: MC1R = pigmentation susceptibility allele; 2 variants → up to 17× BRAF-mutant melanoma.

stem: A patient develops several small red-orange papules ("BAPomas"/MBAITs) showing loss of nuclear BAP1 staining. The associated tumor syndrome predisposes to which constellation?

options.A: Cutaneous and uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma
options.B: Pancreatic carcinoma, astrocytoma, and early-onset cutaneous melanoma
options.C: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and mucosal neuromas
options.D: Basal cell carcinomas with palmar pits, jaw cysts, and medulloblastoma

exp: BAP1 tumor syndrome arises from germline BAP1 (a deubiquitylase) loss and predisposes to cutaneous and uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma (with lesser BCC/meningioma/cholangiocarcinoma risk); the characteristic skin lesions are BAP1-inactivated melanocytomas (MBAITs/"BAPomas") with loss of nuclear BAP1 staining. The distractors are other tumor syndromes: pancreatic cancer + neural tumors fits CDKN2A, MTC + pheochromocytoma is MEN2, and BCCs with palmar pits + medulloblastoma is Gorlin syndrome.
hy: BAP1 syndrome = cutaneous + uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, RCC; MBAITs lose nuclear BAP1.

stem: By the AAD "rule of 3," genetic testing for familial melanoma is indicated for an individual with:

options.A: ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, or a family with ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic-cancer diagnoses on one side
options.B: A single thin invasive melanoma in a person who has no relevant personal or family history of cancer
options.C: Any patient over the age of 40 years who presents with their first single primary invasive melanoma
options.D: Only those patients in whom a pathogenic germline CDKN2A mutation has already been formally confirmed

exp: The "rule of 3" flags those most likely to carry a CDKN2A germline mutation: a personal history of ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, or a family with ≥3 invasive-melanoma and/or pancreatic-cancer diagnoses among close relatives on the same side. The distractors miss this bar: a single melanoma without family history, or any patient simply over 40 with one melanoma, does not meet criteria, and an already-confirmed mutation is not a prerequisite for testing.
hy: Rule of 3 = ≥3 melanomas in one person, or ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic dx on one side of the family.

stem: For genetic risk assessment of familial melanoma, current AAD guidance prefers:

options.A: Multi-gene panel testing rather than single-gene (CDKN2A-only) testing
options.B: Single-gene CDKN2A testing alone as sufficient in essentially all cases
options.C: Routine germline testing of every patient who presents with one melanoma
options.D: Deferring counseling entirely unless a pathogenic mutation is already known

exp: Because single-gene assays miss relevant mutations in other susceptibility genes (and associated internal malignancies), multi-gene panel testing is preferred over CDKN2A-only testing for familial melanoma risk assessment. The distractors over- or under-reach: testing is *not* recommended for every patient with one melanoma, single-gene testing is no longer preferred, and counseling is recommended regardless of whether a mutation is already known.
hy: Use multi-gene panels (not CDKN2A alone) for familial-melanoma genetic testing.

stem: The Nambour (Queensland) randomized trial provided the pivotal evidence that regular sunscreen use:

options.A: Reduced new primary melanoma incidence by ~50–73% at 10-year follow-up
options.B: Had no effect on melanoma but reduced actinic keratosis counts only
options.C: Increased melanoma risk by lowering circulating vitamin D levels
options.D: Lowered melanoma mortality but left overall incidence unchanged

exp: The Nambour Skin Cancer Prevention trial (Queensland, Australia) is the single RCT demonstrating that daily sunscreen prevents melanoma: regular SPF-16 application reduced new primary cutaneous melanoma incidence by ~50–73% measured 10 years after the intervention. The distractors understate or invert this: it showed a genuine melanoma reduction (not AK-only), did not raise risk via vitamin D, and the headline endpoint was incidence, not mortality.
hy: Nambour RCT: daily sunscreen ↓ new melanoma ~50–73% at 10 yr.

stem: Which is the most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma in light-skinned individuals, accounting for ~60–70% of cases and arising most often in the fourth-to-sixth decades?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: Superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) — broadly corresponding to low-CSD melanoma — is the commonest subtype in light skin (~60–70%), diagnosed most often between 40 and 60 years. It begins as a slowly enlarging macule with a radial (horizontal) growth phase before any vertical phase. The distractors are the other classic subtypes with different epidemiology: nodular (~10–25%, 6th decade, de novo vertical growth), lentigo maligna (~10–15%, 7th decade, sun-damaged face), and acral lentiginous (~2–5%, palms/soles/nail).
hy: SSM = most common (~60–70%), low-CSD, radial then vertical growth.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Types of Primary Melanomas, Table 113.5

stem: Which statement about superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) is correct?

options.A: It favors the back in men and the legs in women, arising in a pre-existing nevus in about half of cases
options.B: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor without any preceding horizontal radial growth phase
options.C: It occurs almost exclusively on the chronically sun-damaged facial skin of much older elderly patients
options.D: It is the melanoma subtype most disproportionately frequent in darkly pigmented non-White populations

exp: SSM favors the trunk (especially the back) in men and the legs in women, and is the subtype most likely to arise within a pre-existing nevus — about half in one study (though any single nevus's transformation risk remains tiny). The distractors describe other subtypes: arising de novo as pure vertical growth is nodular melanoma, chronically sun-damaged facial skin of the elderly is LMM, and disproportionate frequency in dark skin is ALM.
hy: SSM: back (men)/legs (women); ~50% arise in a pre-existing nevus.

stem: Which feature distinguishes nodular melanoma (NM) from the other growth-pattern subtypes?

options.A: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor with no preceding radial growth phase
options.B: It is defined by a prolonged radial growth phase confined for years to the epidermis
options.C: It is the subtype most strongly correlated with high cumulative ultraviolet sun damage
options.D: It almost always retains heavy uniform pigmentation and very rarely ulcerates or bleeds

exp: Nodular melanoma is believed to arise de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor, lacking the preceding radial growth phase that characterizes the other types — which is why it presents as a rapidly enlarging papule/nodule and is detected later. The distractors invert this: a prolonged epidermal radial phase describes SSM/LMM, cumulative sun damage characterizes LMM, and nodular melanoma can ulcerate, bleed, and be amelanotic.
hy: NM = de novo vertical growth, no radial phase → presents thick.

stem: Although nodular melanoma represents only ~10–25% of melanomas, why does it contribute disproportionately to melanoma deaths?

options.A: It is detected late and thick — making up ~50% of melanomas >2 mm and ~40% of fatal cases
options.B: It is intrinsically more resistant to systemic chemotherapy than other subtypes at equal stage
options.C: Its melanoma-specific survival is far worse stage-for-stage than that of the other subtypes
options.D: It spreads hematogenously to viscera while the other subtypes metastasize only via lymphatics

exp: Nodular melanoma's outsized mortality is a *detection* problem, not a unique biology: lacking a radial phase, it is found late and thick — making up nearly 50% of melanomas >2 mm at diagnosis and ~40% of ultimately fatal cases. The distractors propose biology that the chapter explicitly does not support: melanoma-specific survival is not clearly worse stage-for-stage, and there is no special chemoresistance or exclusively hematogenous spread.
hy: NM kills via late/thick detection (~40% of fatal cases), not worse stage-for-stage biology.

stem: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM, high-CSD melanoma) characteristically:

options.A: Arises on sun-damaged face/scalp of older adults from a macular in-situ precursor (lentigo maligna)
options.B: Arises on the trunk of young adults who have had intense intermittent recreational sun exposure
options.C: Occurs on the palms, soles, and nail units largely independent of ultraviolet sun exposure
options.D: Presents abruptly as a rapidly growing amelanotic dermal nodule over a few weeks to months

exp: LMM (high-CSD melanoma) develops on chronically photodamaged skin — classically the face and the scalp of balding older men — beginning as a slowly enlarging macular precursor (lentigo maligna = melanoma in situ) before an invasive papule/nodule appears. The distractors describe other subtypes: trunk of young adults with intermittent sun is SSM, acral/nail sites independent of sun is ALM, and a rapid amelanotic nodule is nodular melanoma.
hy: LMM = sun-damaged face/scalp of elderly; macular LM precursor.

stem: Regarding lentigo maligna (LM), which statement is correct?

options.A: LM is melanoma in situ of sun-damaged skin; about 5% of LM lesions progress to invasive melanoma
options.B: LM is by definition an invasive melanoma as soon as it becomes clinically pigmented and visible
options.C: LM is a wholly benign lentigo of photodamaged skin with essentially no malignant potential at all
options.D: Roughly half of all LM lesions progress to frank invasive melanoma within the first year of onset

exp: Lentigo maligna is melanoma in situ arising in sun-damaged skin; roughly 5% of LM lesions are estimated to progress to invasive melanoma, though true rates from the in-situ precursor are unknown. The distractors misstate its nature: LM is in situ (not invasive by definition), it is not benign without malignant potential, and ~50% progression within a year grossly overstates the rate.
hy: LM = melanoma in situ of CSD skin; ~5% progress to invasive.

stem: Which statement about acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is correct?

options.A: Its absolute incidence is similar across races, but it is a far higher proportion of melanomas in Black/Asian patients
options.B: It is caused by cumulative ultraviolet sun exposure delivered directly to the palms and soles over decades
options.C: It is the single most common melanoma subtype overall among non-Hispanic White patients in the SEER data
options.D: It is biologically and genetically identical to superficial spreading melanoma despite its acral location

exp: ALM incidence is roughly similar across racial/ethnic groups, but because darker-skinned people develop few UV-related melanomas, ALM makes up a disproportionate share of their melanomas — up to ~36% in Black and ~22% in Asian patients, versus ~2% in non-Hispanic Whites. The distractors are false: ALM is not UV-driven, it is not the commonest subtype in White patients (that is SSM), and it is a genetically distinct subtype, not identical to SSM.
hy: ALM: similar absolute incidence across races, but up to ~36% of melanomas in Black patients.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Table 113.5

stem: ALM is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage. Which factor most contributes to this delay?

options.A: Primary misdiagnosis (e.g. as a wart or traumatic lesion) occurs in roughly one-third of patients
options.B: ALM grows faster than any other melanoma subtype, outpacing the interval between skin examinations
options.C: ALM is essentially invisible to dermoscopy because of the thick keratin of the acral stratum corneum
options.D: ALM never produces any pigment, so it is uniformly amelanotic and missed on routine inspection

exp: About one-third of ALMs are initially misdiagnosed — e.g. treated as a wart, callus, or traumatic change — causing substantial delay; lack of awareness of acral melanoma and a high biopsy threshold (surgical morbidity at acral sites) compound it. The distractors are unsupported: ALM is not the fastest-growing subtype, dermoscopy (acral parallel patterns) does help, and ALM is usually pigmented (not always amelanotic).
hy: ~1/3 of ALMs are misdiagnosed → diagnostic delay and advanced stage.

stem: By the SEER frequency data, which ordering of the four classic subtypes from most to least common (overall) is correct?

options.A: Superficial spreading > nodular ≈ lentigo maligna > acral lentiginous
options.B: Nodular > superficial spreading > acral lentiginous > lentigo maligna
options.C: Lentigo maligna > superficial spreading > nodular > acral lentiginous
options.D: Acral lentiginous > nodular > lentigo maligna > superficial spreading

exp: Per SEER frequency data (Table 113.5), the descending order is superficial spreading (~68%) > nodular (~16%) ≈ lentigo maligna (~14%) > acral lentiginous (~2%). The distractors scramble this ranking; the key facts are that SSM dominates and ALM is the rarest of the four classic subtypes overall (despite its prominence in darker-skinned patients).

stem: A spindle-cell dermal melanoma stains negative for HMB45, MART-1/Melan-A, and MITF. Which marker is most reliably positive and key to the diagnosis?

options.A: S100 and SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: Cytokeratin AE1/AE3

exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is a spindle-cell tumor in which the usual melanocytic markers HMB45, MART-1/Melan-A, and MITF are often negative — a classic pitfall — so S100 and SOX10 (sensitive for spindled/desmoplastic melanoma) are the markers that establish melanocytic lineage; SOX10's nuclear staining is especially useful. The distractors are the markers that fail in desmoplastic melanoma (HMB45, MART-1) or an epithelial marker (cytokeratin) that would be negative in melanoma.
hy: Desmoplastic melanoma: HMB45/MART-1/MITF often negative → rely on S100/SOX10.

stem: Which set of features is characteristic of desmoplastic melanoma?

options.A: Spindle cells in fibroblastic stroma, frequent neurotropism, NF1 mutation, and possible adjuvant radiotherapy
options.B: Pagetoid epidermal spread of large pigmented epithelioid melanocytes with abundant cytoplasmic melanin
options.C: A dermal dendritic-melanocyte proliferation driven by GNAQ/GNA11, arising as a blue nodule on the scalp
options.D: A KIT-mutant amelanotic tumor of the oral, nasopharyngeal, and anogenital mucosal surfaces

exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is composed of spindled cells within a fibroblastic (desmoplastic) stroma, shows frequent perineural invasion/neurotropism, often carries NF1 mutations, and — when thick with "pure" desmoplastic histology, neurotropism, or head/neck location — may be considered for adjuvant radiotherapy. The distractors describe other entities: pagetoid epithelioid spread is SSM, a GNAQ/GNA11-driven scalp dermal tumor is blue nevus-like melanoma, and a KIT-mutant mucosal tumor is mucosal melanoma.
hy: Desmoplastic = spindle cells + neurotropism + NF1; consider adjuvant RT.

section: Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus
stem: Blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus") most characteristically:

options.A: Is a blue-black scalp dermal nodule with an associated cellular blue nevus and GNAQ/GNA11 (± BAP1) mutations
options.B: Is an indolent intraepidermal lentiginous melanocytic proliferation arising on the palms and soles
options.C: Carries a BRAF V600E mutation and behaves like a low-CSD superficial spreading melanoma of the trunk
options.D: Is the single most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma encountered in prepubertal children

exp: Blue nevus-like melanoma is a rare dermal tumor, most often on the scalp, presenting as a blue-black deep nodule with an associated cellular blue nevus; the blue-nevus component carries GNAQ or GNA11 mutations, with BAP1/SF3B1/EIF1AX changes in the malignant portion, and it behaves aggressively (high recurrence/metastasis). The distractors are wrong subtypes: an indolent acral intraepidermal lesion, a BRAF V600E SSM, and "most common pediatric melanoma" all describe other entities.
hy: Blue nevus-like melanoma = scalp dermal nodule, GNAQ/GNA11 (+BAP1/SF3B1), aggressive.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus

stem: "Primary dermal melanoma" is distinguished from metastatic melanoma chiefly because it:

options.A: Lacks epidermal involvement/regression yet has a favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-yr) despite thick depth
options.B: Always displays a well-developed overlying intraepidermal in-situ component above the dermal tumor
options.C: Pursues a uniformly fatal metastatic course despite its small size and limited Breslow thickness
options.D: Is defined by a Breslow depth under 1 mm with brisk regression and a prominent radial growth phase

exp: Primary dermal melanoma is a dermal-based tumor lacking overlying epidermal involvement and regression; it can mimic metastasis and present with a thick Breslow depth (average 3–9 mm), yet paradoxically carries a relatively favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-year survival) once metastatic disease is excluded clinically/radiologically. The distractors are false: it characteristically *lacks* an in-situ component, is not uniformly fatal, and is not defined by thin (<1 mm) depth.
hy: Primary dermal melanoma: no epidermal component, thick but favorable (80–100% 5-yr).

stem: A uveal (choroidal) melanoma is analyzed for prognosis. Which molecular finding most strongly predicts poor survival?

options.A: Monosomy 3 (loss of a copy of chromosome 3)
options.B: A BRAF V600E activating point mutation
options.C: A germline CDKN2A (p16) gene deletion
options.D: An NTRK1/3 receptor kinase gene fusion

exp: In uveal melanoma, monosomy 3 is the dominant adverse prognostic marker, dropping 5-year survival from ~100% to <50% (worst when combined with chromosome 8 abnormalities). The distractors do not apply to uveal melanoma prognostication: BRAF V600E and CDKN2A are cutaneous-melanoma lesions, and NTRK fusions characterize Spitz tumors.
hy: Uveal melanoma: monosomy 3 → 5-yr survival ~100% to <50%.

stem: Which statement about uveal melanoma is correct?

options.A: It harbors GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, metastasizes to the liver, and is treated with proton-beam radiotherapy
options.B: It responds to immune checkpoint inhibitors essentially as well as cutaneous melanoma of similar stage
options.C: It is driven by BRAF V600E and is therefore treated first-line with combined BRAF and MEK inhibitors
options.D: Its single most frequent site of distant metastasis is the lung rather than the liver or the brain

exp: Uveal melanoma frequently harbors GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, metastasizes preferentially to the liver, and is now treated with eye-sparing proton-beam radiotherapy rather than enucleation. The distractors are false: checkpoint inhibitors do *not* work as well as in cutaneous melanoma (tebentafusp is the approved agent for HLA-A*02:01-positive disease), it is not BRAF-driven, and its dominant metastatic site is liver, not lung.
hy: Uveal melanoma = GNAQ/GNA11, liver mets, proton beam; poor ICI response.

stem: Mucosal melanoma characteristically:

options.A: Is ~1% of melanomas, is amelanotic in up to ~35%, and can carry activating KIT mutations
options.B: Is the most common melanoma subtype encountered in fair-skinned White populations
options.C: Is reliably detected at an early stage because of its accessible mucosal location
options.D: Is defined by a BRAF V600E mutation and carries an excellent overall prognosis

exp: Mucosal melanomas are rare (~1% of melanomas), arise near mucocutaneous junctions, are amelanotic in up to ~35% (complicating diagnosis), and — like ALM — are genetically distinct and may carry activating KIT mutations potentially sensitive to KIT inhibitors. The distractors are false: they are uncommon (not the commonest subtype), are usually advanced at diagnosis (not detected early), and are not defined by BRAF V600E or a good prognosis.
hy: Mucosal melanoma: ~1%, ~35% amelanotic, KIT-mutant, poor prognosis.

stem: A pregnant woman has a suspicious pigmented lesion. Which management statement is correct?

options.A: Biopsy/excision with lidocaine-epinephrine are safe; if SLNB is needed defer to ≥2nd trimester and avoid blue dyes
options.B: All diagnostic biopsies and definitive excisions must be deferred entirely until after the delivery
options.C: Sentinel-node biopsy should be performed immediately in the first trimester using isosulfan blue dye
options.D: Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication to any surgical treatment of a suspected melanoma at all

exp: In pregnancy, both diagnostic biopsy and wide local excision with small amounts of lidocaine/epinephrine are considered safe and should not be delayed; if SLNB is indicated, guidelines advise waiting until at least the second trimester (to avoid general-anesthesia risks), and isosulfan/methylene blue dyes are contraindicated (radiotracer is preferred, or regional ultrasound surveillance). The distractors over-restrict care (deferring all surgery) or recommend unsafe practice (first-trimester SLNB with blue dye).
hy: Pregnancy: biopsy/WLE safe; SLNB ≥2nd trimester; avoid blue dyes.

stem: Which statement about hormones, pregnancy, and melanoma is correct?

options.A: Exogenous hormones have not been shown to raise risk, and higher parity is linked to lower melanoma risk
options.B: Combined oral contraceptive pills roughly double a woman's lifetime risk of developing melanoma
options.C: Pregnancy occurring after a melanoma diagnosis markedly increases the risk of disease recurrence
options.D: Postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy is the single strongest known melanoma risk factor

exp: Exogenous hormones (oral contraceptives, HRT, fertility hormones) have not been shown to increase melanoma risk, and higher parity is actually associated with *decreased* risk; pregnancy itself has not been shown to worsen prognosis in stage 0–III disease. The distractors invert the evidence (OCPs do not double risk, subsequent pregnancy does not markedly raise recurrence, and HRT is not a major risk factor).
hy: Hormones/OCP/HRT not shown to raise melanoma risk; higher parity → lower risk.

stem: Because conventional ABCDE criteria often fail in prepubertal children, modified pediatric criteria were proposed. What does the pediatric "ABCD" stand for?

options.A: Amelanotic; Bleeding/Bump; Color uniformity; De novo/any Diameter
options.B: Asymmetry; Border irregularity; Color variation; Diameter >6 mm
options.C: Age young; Brisk mitoses; Caliber of vessels; Depth of invasion
options.D: Atypia; Breslow thickness; Clark level; Dermoscopy pattern

exp: Because prepubertal melanomas frequently lack conventional ABCDE features, a modified pediatric "ABCD" was proposed: Amelanotic; Bleeding or Bump; Color uniformity; De novo appearance/any Diameter. The distractors are the adult ABCDE (with Diameter >6 mm), or invented mnemonics ("Age/Brisk/Caliber/Depth"; "Atypia/Breslow/Clark/Dermoscopy") that do not exist.
hy: Pediatric ABCD = Amelanotic, Bleeding/Bump, Color uniformity, De novo/any Diameter.

stem: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, what does "D" denote per Bologna, and which letter do patients most often use to recognize melanoma?

options.A: Diameter >5 mm; "E" (evolution/change) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.B: Depth beyond 1 mm; "A" (asymmetry) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.C: Dermoscopy positivity; "C" (color variegation) is the main patient-recognized clue
options.D: Diameter beyond 10 mm; "B" (border irregularity) is the main patient clue

exp: Bologna's ABCDE stands for Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >5 mm, and Evolution; patients most commonly recognize melanoma through "E" — a new lesion or a change in size/color — underscoring evolution as the most useful warning sign. The distractors substitute the wrong "D" (depth, dermoscopy, >10 mm) or the wrong patient-recognized letter.
hy: ABCDE: D = diameter >5 mm; "E" (change) is the key patient clue.

stem: A clinician notices one pigmented lesion on the back that looks strikingly different from all the patient's other "signature" nevi. This recognition principle is termed:

options.A: The "ugly duckling" sign
options.B: The Hutchinson sign
options.C: The "Little Red Riding Hood" sign
options.D: The "EFG" rule

exp: The "ugly duckling" sign is the comparative recognition of a lesion that stands out as a morphologic outlier among a patient's otherwise similar "signature" nevi — a robust, reproducible clue. The distractors are different signs: Hutchinson sign is periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma, the "Little Red Riding Hood" sign is surrounding erythema, and "EFG" (Elevated/Firm/Growing) aids amelanotic nodular melanoma.
hy: Ugly duckling = the outlier lesion unlike the patient's other nevi.

stem: The "EFG" rule is a clinical aid most useful for detecting which melanoma presentation?

options.A: Amelanotic nodular melanoma — Elevated, Firm, and Growing
options.B: Lentigo maligna on the cheek of a sun-damaged elderly man
options.C: Subungual acral melanoma of the thumb with a pigment band
options.D: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ on the upper back

exp: "EFG" — Elevated, Firm, Growing — is the clinical aid for catching amelanotic nodular melanoma, which lacks the pigment-based ABCDE clues and can otherwise be overlooked. The distractors are presentations where pigment/other signs apply: LMM (ABCDE/dermoscopy), subungual melanoma (Hutchinson sign), and SSM in situ (ABCDE).
hy: EFG (Elevated/Firm/Growing) → amelanotic nodular melanoma.

stem: Clinical (naked-eye) diagnostic accuracy for melanoma and the effect of dermoscopy are best described as:

options.A: ~75% by visual inspection alone, rising to ~90% with expert dermoscopy use
options.B: ~95% by visual inspection alone, with expert dermoscopy adding essentially nothing
options.C: ~50% by visual inspection alone, left entirely unchanged by the use of dermoscopy
options.D: ~30% by visual inspection alone, paradoxically falling further when dermoscopy is used

exp: Naked-eye clinical diagnosis of melanoma is correct in no more than ~75% of cases, rising to ~90% in expert hands using dermoscopy (which also prevents unnecessary biopsies); even so, 10–25% of melanomas may be missed ("featureless" melanoma). The distractors over- or under-state these figures or claim dermoscopy is useless/harmful.
hy: Visual accuracy ~75% → ~90% with expert dermoscopy.

stem: In the two-step dermoscopic algorithm, step 1 asks whether a lesion is melanocytic. Which finding qualifies a lesion as melanocytic?

options.A: A pigment network, aggregated globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, or an acral parallel pattern
options.B: Arborizing (branched) telangiectasias radiating across an otherwise pearly translucent papule
options.C: Multiple milia-like cysts together with comedo-like openings in a "stuck-on" verrucous plaque
options.D: Sharply demarcated red-blue lacunae corresponding to dilated vascular spaces in the dermis

exp: Step 1 classifies a lesion as melanocytic if it shows at least one of: a pigment network, aggregated globules, streaks, homogeneous blue pigment, or (on acral skin) a parallel pattern. The distractors are features of *non-melanocytic* tumors: arborizing vessels (BCC), milia-like cysts/comedo-like openings (seborrheic keratosis), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion).
hy: Melanocytic clues = network, globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, acral parallel pattern.

stem: Which local dermoscopic feature most specifically raises concern for melanoma?

options.A: A blue-white veil
options.B: Comma-shaped vessels
options.C: A regular narrow network
options.D: Multiple milia-like cysts

exp: Blue-white veil — confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless pigmentation — is among the most specific local dermoscopic signs of melanoma (histologically, acanthotic epidermis with hypergranulosis over heavily pigmented dermal melanocytes). The distractors point elsewhere: comma vessels and a regular narrow network indicate benign lesions, and milia-like cysts indicate seborrheic keratosis.
hy: Blue-white veil = high-specificity melanoma clue.

stem: On dermoscopy, which structure-to-histology correlation is correct?

options.A: Streaks correspond to confluent junctional nests of melanocytes at the lesion margin
options.B: Blue-white veil corresponds to dilated, blood-filled vascular spaces in the upper dermis
options.C: Milia-like cysts correspond to pigmented basaloid-cell aggregates in the papillary dermis
options.D: Regression structures correspond to elongated, regular, evenly pigmented rete ridges

exp: Streaks correspond histologically to confluent junctional nests of melanocytes, an irregular-distribution sign of melanoma. The distractors are mismatched correlates: blue-white veil reflects pigmented dermal melanocytes (not dilated vessels), milia-like cysts are intraepidermal horn pseudocysts of seborrheic keratosis (not basaloid aggregates), and regression structures reflect dermal fibrosis/melanophages (not regular rete ridges).
hy: Streaks = confluent junctional nests.

stem: On dermoscopy of a non-melanocytic lesion, arborizing (branched) vessels are the hallmark of which tumor?

options.A: Basal cell carcinoma
options.B: A seborrheic keratosis
options.C: A cherry hemangioma
options.D: Amelanotic melanoma

exp: Arborizing (branched, tree-like) telangiectasias are the dermoscopic hallmark of basal cell carcinoma. The distractors carry their own signatures: seborrheic keratosis shows milia-like cysts/comedo-like openings (and hairpin vessels), hemangiomas show red-blue lacunae, and melanoma shows atypical/dotted-irregular vessels with blue-white veil.
hy: Arborizing vessels = BCC; each non-melanocytic tumor has its own vascular pattern.

stem: Two non-invasive imaging tools are FDA-approved for melanoma. Which pairing of test and performance is correct?

options.A: RCM ~92% sensitivity/~70% specificity; electrical impedance spectroscopy >96% sensitivity/~35% specificity
options.B: Both reflectance confocal microscopy and impedance spectroscopy exceed 95% specificity and replace histopathology
options.C: Electrical impedance spectroscopy has the higher specificity of the two and rarely yields false-positive results
options.D: Reflectance confocal microscopy images only structures lying below the reticular dermis and subcutis

exp: Two non-invasive tools are FDA-approved: reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM; pooled sensitivity ~92%, specificity ~70%, imaging epidermis/papillary dermis like in vivo histology) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS; sensitivity >96% but specificity only ~35%, with frequent seborrheic-keratosis false positives). The distractors are false: neither replaces histopathology, RCM (not EIS) has the higher specificity, and RCM images the superficial skin, not below the reticular dermis.
hy: RCM ~92%/~70%; EIS >96%/~35% (low specificity).

stem: Which histopathologic feature favors melanoma over a benign melanocytic nevus?

options.A: Absence of maturation — dermal nests and nuclei fail to become smaller with descent
options.B: Architectural symmetry with sharp, well-defined lateral circumscription of the lesion
options.C: Progressive maturation, with smaller nests and nuclei in the deeper dermal component
options.D: Evenly spaced, uniformly sized and shaped junctional nests along the rete ridges

exp: A defining histologic feature of melanoma is *absence of maturation* — dermal nests and melanocyte nuclei fail to become smaller with progressive descent — alongside asymmetry, poor circumscription, and pagetoid scatter. The distractors describe the benign-nevus pattern: symmetry, sharp circumscription, progressive maturation, and evenly spaced uniform nests.
hy: Melanoma = no maturation with descent (+ asymmetry, pagetoid scatter).

stem: In the two-phase model of melanoma growth, which statement is correct?

options.A: The vertical growth phase (dermal nests larger than intraepidermal cells) confers metastatic potential
options.B: The radial (horizontal) growth phase is the phase that confers metastatic potential, even when in situ
options.C: Pagetoid scatter of single melanocytes through the epidermis is what defines the vertical growth phase
options.D: Growth phase (radial vs vertical) is a required, scored component of current AJCC-8 melanoma staging

exp: The vertical growth phase — dermal nests/nodules of melanocytes that are larger than and/or cytologically distinct from the intraepidermal cells — is the phase postulated to carry metastatic potential, whereas the radial growth phase generally lacks it even with focal dermal invasion. The distractors invert this (radial phase is not the metastatic one), misdefine pagetoid scatter (an intraepidermal in-situ feature), or wrongly claim growth phase is part of AJCC staging (it is not).
hy: VGP = metastatic potential; RGP generally lacks it; growth phase is not in AJCC staging.

stem: Which immunohistochemical marker is most sensitive for melanocytes (including spindled/desmoplastic melanoma) and is favored for its easily interpreted nuclear staining?

options.A: SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: CD68

exp: SOX10, a nuclear transcription factor, is highly sensitive for melanocytes — including the spindled cells of desmoplastic melanoma — and is favored because its nuclear pattern is easier to interpret with less background than S100 (though neither is fully melanocyte-specific). The distractors are less suitable: HMB45 and MART-1 are less sensitive and often negative in desmoplastic melanoma, and CD68 is a histiocyte marker.
hy: SOX10 = sensitive nuclear melanocyte marker (catches desmoplastic), easier than S100.

stem: PRAME immunohistochemistry is useful in melanocytic lesions because:

options.A: It is usually negative in benign nevi but diffusely nuclear-positive in most melanomas (desmoplastic/spitzoid excepted)
options.B: It is positive in essentially all benign nevi while remaining negative in the majority of true melanomas
options.C: It reliably stains desmoplastic melanoma in nearly every case, making it the marker of choice for that subtype
options.D: It is a cytoplasmic marker that is specific for keratinocytes rather than for cells of melanocytic lineage

exp: PRAME (PReferentially expressed Antigen in MElanoma) is generally negative in benign nevi/solar lentigines but shows diffuse nuclear positivity in most primary melanomas and metastases, aiding margin assessment and micrometastasis detection; notable exceptions are desmoplastic melanoma (only ~1/3 positive) and spitzoid neoplasms (less reliable). The distractors invert the staining pattern, overstate desmoplastic positivity, or miscall it a keratinocyte marker.
hy: PRAME: negative in nevi, positive in most melanoma; weak in desmoplastic/spitzoid.

stem: Which statement about molecular tests used to classify ambiguous melanocytic lesions is correct?

options.A: CGH screens the whole genome for copy-number change, whereas FISH targets a few loci (6p25/RREB1, 11q13/CCND1, 9p21)
options.B: FISH provides broad genome-wide, hypothesis-free copy-number screening across all chromosomes simultaneously
options.C: CGH reliably detects single-nucleotide point mutations as well as balanced reciprocal translocations and fusions
options.D: Gene expression profiling works by sequencing the entire genome of the tumor to identify its driver mutations

exp: CGH provides genome-wide, hypothesis-free screening for DNA copy-number gains and losses, whereas FISH interrogates only a limited set of preselected loci (e.g. 6p25/RREB1, 6q23/MYB, 11q13/CCND1, 9p21/CDKN2A). The distractors invert their roles (FISH is targeted, not genome-wide), overstate CGH (it misses point mutations and balanced translocations), or misdefine GEP (it measures mRNA expression of selected genes, not whole-genome sequencing).
hy: CGH = genome-wide copy number; FISH = a few targeted loci.

stem: In distinguishing Spitz neoplasms from melanoma, which molecular finding actually favors a Spitz lesion rather than conventional melanoma?

options.A: An isolated gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (BAP1)
options.B: A homozygous deletion of the 9p21 (CDKN2A) tumor-suppressor locus by FISH testing
options.C: A TERT promoter mutation, which is associated with extranodal metastatic spread
options.D: A BRAF V600E mutation accompanied by multiple chromosomal copy-number changes

exp: About 20% of Spitz nevi show an isolated 11p gain (HRAS activation) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (BAP1) — aberrations not typical of conventional melanoma — so these findings actually support a Spitz lesion. The distractors point toward malignancy/aggressiveness instead: homozygous 9p21 (CDKN2A) deletion, TERT promoter mutation, and BRAF V600E with multiple copy-number changes favor melanoma.
hy: Isolated 11p (HRAS) gain or chr-3/BAP1 loss favors Spitz, not melanoma.

stem: In spitzoid neoplasms, which molecular finding is most associated with aggressive behavior (advanced locoregional disease or death)?

options.A: Homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A)
options.B: Gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS)
options.C: Isolated loss of chromosome 3
options.D: A kinase fusion without copy-number change

exp: In spitzoid neoplasms, homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A) is the molecular finding most associated with aggressive behavior — advanced locoregional disease or death — and adding the 9p21/CEP9 FISH probe boosts sensitivity for spitzoid melanoma. The distractors are lower-risk or Spitz-favoring: 11p (HRAS) gain and isolated chr-3 loss favor benignity, and gains of 8q24/11q13 carry lower risk than 9p21 loss.
hy: Homozygous 9p21 (CDKN2A) deletion = aggressive spitzoid behavior.

stem: Subungual melanoma may present with longitudinal melanonychia. Pigment extending onto the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium is termed:

options.A: The Hutchinson sign
options.B: The ugly-duckling sign
options.C: The Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: The EFG sign

exp: The Hutchinson sign is periungual extension of pigment from a longitudinal melanonychia band onto the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium, a worrying clue for subungual melanoma. The distractors are unrelated signs: ugly duckling (outlier nevus), Little Red Riding Hood (surrounding erythema), and EFG (Elevated/Firm/Growing amelanotic nodular melanoma).
hy: Hutchinson sign = periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma.

stem: A fair-skinned 50-year-old has this flat-to-slightly-raised lesion on the back showing asymmetry, irregular notched borders, and brown-black color variation. Which melanoma subtype does the morphology best represent?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: The flat, asymmetric lesion with irregular notched borders and brown-black color variation on the trunk is the classic look of superficial spreading melanoma (low-CSD), the commonest subtype. The same-class distractors are the other growth-pattern subtypes, distinguished by morphology/site: nodular is a raised dome, lentigo maligna is a facial macule of sun-damaged skin, and acral lentiginous is on palm/sole/nail.
hy: Flat asymmetric trunk lesion with color variation = SSM.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.8, Superficial Spreading Melanoma

stem: This darkly pigmented dome-shaped papule developed rapidly over weeks. Which subtype is most consistent with the appearance?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Dysplastic nevus

exp: A darkly pigmented dome-shaped papule developing rapidly over weeks is typical of nodular melanoma, which grows vertically from the outset. The distractors are other subtypes: SSM is flat with a radial phase, lentigo maligna is a facial macule, and a dysplastic nevus is a flatter, more symmetric benign lesion.
hy: Rapidly growing pigmented nodule = nodular melanoma.

stem: This asymmetric pigmented macule/plaque is on the face of an elderly man with chronic sun damage. Which subtype does it represent?

options.A: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Nodular melanoma

exp: An asymmetric pigmented macule on the chronically sun-damaged face of an elderly person is the classic presentation of lentigo maligna melanoma (high-CSD). The distractors differ by site/biology: SSM favors the trunk of younger adults, ALM is acral, and nodular melanoma is a raised rapidly growing nodule.
hy: Pigmented facial macule in elderly CSD skin = LMM.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.13, Lentigo Maligna Melanoma

stem: This irregularly pigmented lesion is on the plantar surface. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus-like melanoma

exp: An irregularly pigmented lesion on the plantar (sole) surface is the classic site for acral lentiginous melanoma. The same-class distractors are the other subtypes, which favor different sites (SSM trunk, LMM face, blue nevus-like melanoma the scalp).
hy: Pigmented plantar lesion = acral lentiginous melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.15, Acral Lentiginous Melanoma

stem: This nail shows a pigmented band with extension of pigment onto the proximal nail fold. The periungual extension is which clinical sign?

options.A: Hutchinson sign
options.B: Ugly-duckling sign
options.C: Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: EFG sign

exp: Pigment extending from the nail band onto the proximal nail fold is the Hutchinson sign, a clue to subungual melanoma. The distractors are unrelated signs (ugly duckling = outlier nevus; Little Red Riding Hood = surrounding erythema; EFG = elevated/firm/growing amelanotic nodule).
hy: Periungual pigment spread = Hutchinson sign (subungual melanoma).

stem: This skin-colored to light-pink nodule lacked obvious pigment; biopsy confirmed melanoma 4 mm thick. Which descriptor fits?

options.A: Amelanotic melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ
options.D: Dermal nevus

exp: A skin-colored to light-pink nodule lacking obvious pigment that proves to be a thick melanoma is an amelanotic melanoma — easily mistaken for a benign nodule and a reason for the EFG rule. The distractors are pigmented or benign: LMM and SSM in situ are pigmented macular lesions, and a dermal nevus is benign.
hy: Non-pigmented melanoma nodule = amelanotic melanoma.

stem: This blue-black, deeply situated nodule arose on the scalp; histology showed an associated cellular blue nevus. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Blue nevus-like melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma arising de novo
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Lentigo maligna melanoma

exp: A blue-black deep nodule on the scalp with an associated cellular blue nevus is blue nevus-like melanoma. The same-class distractors are other subtypes differing by site/origin: de novo nodular melanoma lacks the blue-nevus association, and ALM/LMM occur at acral/facial sites.
hy: Blue-black scalp nodule + cellular blue nevus = blue nevus-like melanoma.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.19, Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus

stem: This dermoscopic image of a melanoma shows a confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless area. What is this feature called?

options.A: Blue-white veil
options.B: Milia-like cysts
options.C: Arborizing vessels
options.D: Red-blue lacunae

exp: The confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless zone is the blue-white veil, a high-specificity dermoscopic melanoma feature. The same-class distractors are other dermoscopic structures with different "homes": milia-like cysts (seborrheic keratosis), arborizing vessels (BCC), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion).
hy: Confluent blue-white structureless area = blue-white veil.

stem: This H&E section shows atypical melanocytes as solitary units and irregular nests scattered at all levels of the epidermis. This pattern is termed:

options.A: Pagetoid scatter
options.B: Maturation with descent
options.C: Kamino bodies
options.D: Cowdry inclusions

exp: Atypical melanocytes scattered as solitary units and irregular nests at *all* levels of the epidermis define pagetoid scatter, a hallmark of melanoma (in situ when confined to the epidermis). The distractors are different concepts: maturation with descent is a benign-nevus feature, Kamino bodies are seen in Spitz nevi, and Cowdry inclusions are viral.
hy: Upward (all-level) intraepidermal spread = pagetoid scatter.

stem: This H&E section from sun-damaged facial skin shows a lentiginous proliferation of single atypical melanocytes along the basal layer with extension down hair follicles and marked dermal solar elastosis. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Lentigo maligna
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Nodular melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: A lentiginous proliferation of single atypical basal melanocytes with extension down follicular epithelium plus marked dermal solar elastosis is the histology of lentigo maligna (high-CSD melanoma in situ). The same-class distractors are other subtypes' histology: SSM shows broad pagetoid spread without prominent elastosis, nodular melanoma is a dermal nodule, and ALM shows lentiginous spread in a thick acral epidermis.
hy: Lentiginous single-cell spread + adnexal extension + solar elastosis = lentigo maligna.

stem: This dermal tumor shows loosely textured spindle cells within a fibroblastic stroma with scattered lymphoid aggregates; HMB45 and MART-1 were negative but S100 was positive. Which diagnosis fits?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus

exp: Loosely textured spindle cells in a fibroblastic stroma with lymphoid aggregates, negative for HMB45/MART-1 but S100-positive, is desmoplastic melanoma — the IHC profile is the giveaway and a classic pitfall. The same-class distractors are other melanocytic lesions that do *not* fit a spindle-cell S100-only profile: nodular and superficial spreading melanoma are epithelioid and usually marker-positive, and blue nevus is benign dendritic/pigmented.
hy: Spindle cells + fibrous stroma + S100+/HMB45−/MART-1− = desmoplastic melanoma.

stem: This H&E section shows a relatively well-circumscribed dermal nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with minimal overlying epidermal involvement. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna
options.C: Melanoma in situ
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

exp: A relatively well-circumscribed dermal nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with little overlying epidermal involvement is the histology of nodular melanoma (vertical growth, minimal radial phase). The same-class distractors differ histologically: lentigo maligna and melanoma in situ are intraepidermal processes, and ALM shows lentiginous spread in a thick acral epidermis.
hy: Dermal nodule, scant epidermal component = nodular melanoma histology.

stem: On the dorsal foot of an 84-year-old man, this asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule was the "outlier" lesion compared with the patient's other nevi. The lesion is best classified as:

options.A: Melanoma in situ
options.B: Invasive nodular melanoma
options.C: Solar lentigo
options.D: Dermatofibroma

exp: A subtle, asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule that is the morphologic "outlier" among the patient's nevi is melanoma in situ — small early melanomas are easily overlooked, and dermoscopy aids detection. The distractors differ: an invasive nodular melanoma would be a raised nodule, and a solar lentigo or dermatofibroma would be a benign, well-defined lesion.
hy: Subtle outlier macule on sun-damaged skin = melanoma in situ.

stem: Which single histologic feature of a primary cutaneous melanoma is the strongest predictor of survival?

options.A: Breslow tumor thickness
options.B: Clark level of invasion
options.C: Dermal mitotic rate
options.D: Presence of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes

exp: Breslow thickness — the vertical extent of the tumor in millimeters — is the strongest single histologic predictor of survival and the primary driver of T-staging. The distractors are weaker or superseded: Clark level (anatomic level of invasion) was largely abandoned, dermal mitotic rate is prognostic but no longer upstages, and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes are a secondary feature.
hy: Breslow depth = strongest histologic survival predictor.

stem: This schematic illustrates how melanoma thickness is measured. The double-headed arrow (the Breslow measurement) is taken from which landmark to which?

options.A: From the top of the granular layer (or ulcer base) to the deepest tumor cell
options.B: From the outer stratum corneum surface down to the dermal–subcutaneous junction
options.C: From the dermal–epidermal junction down to the deepest invasive tumor cell
options.D: By the anatomic level of invasion reached (papillary versus reticular dermis)

exp: The Breslow method measures from the top of the granular cell layer of the epidermis (or, if ulcerated, from the base of the ulcer) to the deepest point of tumor invasion, using an ocular micrometer. The distractors misplace the landmarks (whole-skin thickness, the dermal–epidermal junction) or describe the *Clark level* system (anatomic levels), which is a different, largely abandoned method.
hy: Breslow = granular layer (or ulcer base) → deepest tumor cell.

stem: In AJCC-8, which Breslow thickness boundaries separate the T categories?
exp: AJCC-8 T-category thickness boundaries are T1 ≤1.0 mm, T2 >1.0–2.0 mm, T3 >2.0–4.0 mm, and T4 >4.0 mm — the 1/2/4 mm breakpoints anchor the whole system. The distractors use adjacent but wrong cut-points (0.8/1.6/3.0; 2/4/6; 1/3/5), which is exactly the trap on a numeric staging item — you must recall the exact figures.
hy: T boundaries: 1, 2, 4 mm.

stem: Within T1, what distinguishes T1a from T1b in AJCC-8?

options.A: T1a = <0.8 mm no ulceration; T1b = <0.8 mm with ulceration OR 0.8–1.0 mm
options.B: T1a = <1.0 mm with any dermal mitoses; T1b = <1.0 mm without any mitoses
options.C: T1a = Clark anatomic level II invasion; T1b = Clark anatomic level III invasion
options.D: T1a = <0.5 mm thickness; T1b = 0.5–1.0 mm thickness, regardless of ulceration

exp: Within T1, AJCC-8 splits by a 0.8 mm threshold and ulceration: T1a is <0.8 mm without ulceration, while T1b is <0.8 mm WITH ulceration OR 0.8–1.0 mm (with or without ulceration). The distractors use criteria that AJCC-8 dropped or never used here — mitotic rate (removed as a T1 splitter), Clark level, or a 0.5 mm cut-point.
hy: T1a <0.8 mm no ulcer; T1b <0.8 mm+ulcer OR 0.8–1.0 mm.

stem: How does ulceration affect AJCC T-staging, and how is it defined histologically?

options.A: It upstages a tumor "a"→"b" within its thickness band; defined as loss of intact epidermis + host response
options.B: It lowers the assigned T category by one full level because it represents earlier, more superficial disease
options.C: It is determined purely clinically as a visibly eroded or crusted surface, without any histologic criterion
options.D: It has no effect on T-staging in AJCC-8 and is recorded only as an optional descriptive comment

exp: Ulceration — defined histologically as absence of an intact epidermis over a major portion of the tumor plus a host response (fibrin, inflammation) — upstages a tumor from the "a" to the "b" subcategory within its thickness band, reflecting worse prognosis. The distractors are false: it does not lower the T category, it is a histologic (not purely clinical) determination, and it definitely affects staging.
hy: Ulceration (histologic) = a→b upstaging within each T.

stem: What is the current status of dermal mitotic rate in AJCC-8 melanoma staging?

options.A: It is prognostic but no longer upstages the primary (e.g. ≥11/mm² lowers 5-yr survival to ~84%)
options.B: It is a required staging component that directly defines the T-stage of every primary melanoma
options.C: A mitotic rate of ≥1/mm² now automatically reclassifies a T1a tumor as a higher-risk T1b tumor
options.D: It has been shown to carry no prognostic value at all and is therefore no longer reported by pathologists

exp: Dermal mitotic rate was a T1 splitter in the prior (AJCC-7) system but in AJCC-8 it no longer upstages the primary tumor; it remains an independent prognostic factor (e.g. ≥11/mm² lowers 5-year survival to ~84% in node-negative disease). The distractors overstate its current staging role (defining T-stage, auto-converting T1a→T1b) or wrongly deny any prognostic value.
hy: Mitotic rate: prognostic but no longer upstages in AJCC-8.

stem: Which factor is the principal determinant of the N category?

options.A: Number of tumor-involved nodes (1; 2–3; ≥4) plus occult-vs-detected status and satellite/in-transit disease
options.B: The Breslow thickness of the primary tumor measured from the granular layer to the deepest cell
options.C: The serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level measured at the time of the initial staging work-up
options.D: The specific anatomic site of any distant metastases, such as skin, lung, liver, or central nervous system

exp: The N category is driven by the number of tumor-involved regional nodes (1; 2–3; ≥4), whether involvement is clinically occult (microscopic, SLN-detected) versus clinically detected, and the presence of satellite/in-transit/microsatellite disease. The distractors belong to other categories: Breslow thickness drives T, LDH and distant-metastasis site drive M.
hy: N = node number (1/2–3/≥4) + occult vs detected + satellite/in-transit.

stem: How are satellite and in-transit metastases defined and prognostically weighted in AJCC-8?

options.A: Satellites are ≤2 cm from the primary, in-transit lesions >2 cm but not past the nodes; both are lymphatic, similar prognosis
options.B: Satellites are >2 cm from the primary tumor whereas in-transit lesions lie within 2 cm of the excision scar
options.C: Both satellite and in-transit lesions are hematogenous visceral deposits and are classified as M1 distant disease
options.D: In-transit metastases carry a markedly better prognosis than satellite lesions despite both being lymphatic

exp: Satellite metastases lie within 2 cm of the primary and in-transit metastases lie >2 cm away but not beyond the regional nodes; AJCC-8 considers the 2 cm cut-off arbitrary because both are lymphatic spread with similar (poor) prognosis. The distractors reverse the distances, miscall them hematogenous M1 disease, or wrongly split their prognosis.
hy: Satellite ≤2 cm, in-transit >2 cm; both lymphatic, similar prognosis.

stem: A patient has in-transit metastases but no tumor-involved lymph nodes. Which N category applies?

options.A: N1c
options.B: N0
options.C: N2a
options.D: N3b

exp: N1c denotes in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with NO tumor-involved regional nodes — the "c" suffix flags lymphatic non-nodal disease. The distractors are wrong: N0 means no regional disease, N2a is 2–3 occult nodes, and N3b involves ≥4 nodes/matted nodes.
hy: N1c = satellite/in-transit disease with no involved nodes.

stem: In AJCC-8 M classification, which mapping of metastatic site to M category is correct?

options.A: M1a = skin/soft tissue/non-regional node; M1b = lung; M1c = non-CNS visceral; M1d = CNS
options.B: M1a = lung; M1b = liver; M1c = brain; M1d = bone marrow and the skeletal system
options.C: M1a = CNS; M1b = visceral organs; M1c = lung; M1d = skin and distant soft tissue
options.D: M1a = any visceral organ; M1b = skin only; M1c = node only; M1d = lung only
