exp: AJCC-8 M-category maps by anatomic site: M1a = distant skin/soft tissue/non-regional node, M1b = lung, M1c = other (non-CNS) visceral sites, M1d = CNS — with an LDH suffix (0)/(1). The distractors scramble these site assignments.
hy: M1a skin/soft tissue, M1b lung, M1c visceral, M1d CNS.

stem: What is the role of serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in melanoma staging?

options.A: It subclassifies stage IV (the M-category suffix) and predicts survival in advanced disease
options.B: It defines the thickness boundary that separates T2 tumors from thicker T3 tumors
options.C: It is recommended as a routine baseline screening test for all newly diagnosed melanomas
options.D: It is the laboratory value that determines whether sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated

exp: Since 2002, LDH has been a standard component of stage IV subclassification (the M-category suffix), as elevated LDH predicts worse survival and treatment response in advanced disease. The distractors misuse it: LDH does not define T2/T3 boundaries, is not recommended as a baseline screen in early disease (low sensitivity/specificity), and does not determine SLNB indications.
hy: LDH = stage IV M-suffix; advanced-disease survival marker.

stem: Which description of the AJCC-8 stage groupings is correct?

options.A: Stage 0 = in situ; I–II = localized; III = regional nodal/lymphatic; IV = distant metastasis
options.B: Stage 0 = localized invasive disease; stage I = in situ; III = distant; IV = nodal metastasis
options.C: Stage I = regional nodal disease; II = in-transit metastasis; III = in situ; IV = localized
options.D: Stage II = distant metastasis; III = melanoma in situ; IV = localized invasive primary tumor

exp: Stage 0 is melanoma in situ; stages I–II are localized invasive cutaneous disease; stage III is regional nodal and/or lymphatic (satellite/in-transit) metastasis; stage IV is distant metastasis. The distractors scramble these definitions.
hy: 0 in situ · I–II localized · III regional · IV distant.

stem: Which set of AJCC-8 5-year melanoma-specific survival figures is correct?

options.A: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~82%; stage IIID ~32%
options.B: Stage IA ~75%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~50%; stage IIID ~10%
options.C: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~99%; stage IIID ~80%
options.D: Stage IA ~60%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~40%; stage IIID ~5%

exp: AJCC-8 survival anchors: stage IA ~99% 5-year melanoma-specific survival, falling to ~82% for a thick ulcerated node-negative tumor (T4b N0), down to ~32% for stage IIID — illustrating the steep prognostic gradient. The distractors are uniformly wrong magnitudes (too pessimistic for early disease or too optimistic for advanced).

stem: Once nodal status is known, which factor is described as the strongest single predictor of survival and recurrence in primary cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: The pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node(s)
options.B: The patient's chronologic age at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The anatomic body site of the primary melanoma
options.D: The maximum clinical diameter of the primary lesion

exp: The pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node(s) is repeatedly identified as the strongest single predictor of survival and recurrence for primary cutaneous melanoma ≥1 mm thick — the reason SLNB is offered for staging. The distractors (age, site, diameter) are genuine but weaker prognostic variables.
hy: SLN status = strongest survival/recurrence predictor.

stem: In localized (stage I/II) melanoma, which clinical variables independently worsen prognosis?

options.A: Male sex, older age, and a trunk/head/neck (rather than extremity) primary site
options.B: Female sex together with a distal extremity location of the primary tumor
options.C: Younger patient age combined with a scalp location, but only in women
options.D: Left-sided versus right-sided location of the primary cutaneous tumor

exp: In localized disease, independent adverse clinical variables are male sex, older age, and a trunk/head/neck location (scalp particularly poor) — extremity tumors and female sex fare better. The distractors invert these associations or invent irrelevant ones (laterality).
hy: Worse in localized melanoma: male sex, older age, trunk/head/neck site.

stem: Among stage III patients, which nodal feature confers a worse prognosis?

options.A: Clinically detectable (palpable, macroscopic) nodal disease rather than occult microscopic disease
options.B: Microscopic disease detected only on sentinel-node biopsy rather than clinically palpable adenopathy
options.C: A single tumor-involved regional lymph node rather than four or more tumor-involved regional nodes
options.D: Clinically occult disease without satellites rather than the presence of multiple matted regional nodes

exp: Among node-positive (stage III) patients, clinically detectable (palpable, macroscopic) nodal metastasis carries a worse prognosis than clinically occult (microscopic, SLN-detected) disease — nodal tumor burden matters. The distractors invert the burden relationship or compare the wrong pair (fewer vs more nodes is true but is not the occult-vs-detected axis asked).
hy: Macroscopic (palpable) nodes worse than occult (microscopic) disease.

stem: In stage IV melanoma, which is the major prognostic factor, and what was the historical outlook before modern therapy?

options.A: Site of distant mets (visceral worse); historically ~9-month median survival, <10% 5-yr with elevated LDH
options.B: Primary tumor diameter; historically a 5-year survival of roughly 50% even with visceral spread present
options.C: Patient biologic sex; historically a median survival of about 5 years regardless of metastatic burden
options.D: The number of separate primary melanomas; historically an overall cure rate of approximately 80%

exp: In stage IV, the dominant prognostic factor is the site of distant metastases — visceral disease is worse than non-visceral (skin/subcutaneous/distant node) — and historically (pre-modern therapy) median survival was ~9 months with <10% 5-year survival when LDH was elevated. The distractors cite wrong factors/figures.
hy: Stage IV: visceral worse; historical median ~9 mo, <10% 5-yr if LDH high.

stem: What do guidelines recommend regarding baseline imaging/laboratory tests in an asymptomatic patient with newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: They recommend AGAINST routine baseline imaging or labs at any thickness in asymptomatic patients
options.B: They advise whole-body FDG PET-CT for every newly diagnosed patient regardless of tumor thickness
options.C: They advise a baseline contrast-enhanced brain MRI for all patients with T1 (≤1 mm) melanomas
options.D: They advise routine baseline serum LDH and a staging CT scan for all melanoma in-situ lesions

exp: Evidence-based guidelines uniformly recommend AGAINST routine baseline imaging or labs in asymptomatic patients with cutaneous melanoma of ANY thickness, because synchronous distant disease is rare and unfocused testing yields false positives. The distractors prescribe exactly the over-testing the guidelines reject.
hy: No baseline imaging/labs in asymptomatic melanoma, any thickness.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Per NCCN/AAD guidance, surveillance cross-sectional imaging (e.g. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET-CT) in asymptomatic patients may be considered starting at which stage?

options.A: Stage IIB and higher
options.B: Stage 0 (in situ)
options.C: Stage IA
options.D: Only after distant metastasis is confirmed

exp: NCCN/AAD guidance considers surveillance imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast or whole-body FDG PET-CT) in asymptomatic patients with stage IIB melanoma and higher, i.e. those at higher recurrence risk. The distractors apply imaging to low-risk disease (in situ, IA) or restrict it until metastasis is already proven.
hy: Surveillance imaging considered from stage IIB upward.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Which imaging modality has the highest sensitivity for detecting distant metastatic melanoma at staging?

options.A: Whole-body FDG PET-CT (~80% sensitivity for distant disease)
options.B: Plain chest radiography of the thorax in two projections
options.C: A plain-film skeletal radiographic survey of the axial bones
options.D: Regional nodal-basin ultrasound (best for nodal, not distant, disease)

exp: Whole-body FDG PET-CT has the highest sensitivity (~80%) and specificity (~87%) for distant metastasis at staging/surveillance, making it the preferred modality when available. The distractors are weaker: chest radiography lacks sensitivity, skeletal survey is not a staging tool, and nodal ultrasound is best for *nodal* (not distant) disease.
hy: PET-CT = best for distant mets (~80% sens); nodal US best for nodes.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

stem: Why is routine serum LDH not recommended in lower-stage melanoma?

options.A: It has low sensitivity and specificity for detecting metastasis in early-stage disease
options.B: It is far too costly a test to justify ordering in routine lower-stage follow-up
options.C: It becomes elevated only in melanoma in situ and not in invasive or metastatic disease
options.D: It reliably replaces sentinel-node biopsy for nodal staging and so is redundant here

exp: Routine LDH is not recommended in lower-stage melanoma because it has low sensitivity and specificity for detecting metastasis; its established role is stage IV subclassification (with S100B used in Europe for higher-risk surveillance). The distractors are false (cost is not the reason; LDH is not in-situ-specific; it does not replace SLNB).
hy: LDH low sens/spec early → reserved for stage IV.

stem: Which imaging modality is preferred for evaluating suspected brain metastases in melanoma?

options.A: MRI with contrast
options.B: Chest radiography
options.C: Nodal-basin ultrasound
options.D: Bone scan

exp: MRI with contrast is preferred for evaluating brain metastases (and select soft-tissue operability questions); CT with contrast is an alternative. The distractors are inappropriate for brain assessment (chest radiograph, nodal ultrasound, bone scan).
hy: Brain metastases → contrast MRI (preferred).

stem: What is the preferred initial biopsy of a lesion suspected to be melanoma, and why?

options.A: Excisional/complete biopsy with narrow (1–3 mm) margins and adequate depth, allowing accurate microstaging
options.B: A superficial shave biopsy sampling only the single most darkly pigmented portion of the lesion
options.C: Fine-needle aspiration of the lesion to obtain cells for cytologic and immunocytochemical analysis
options.D: An incisional wedge biopsy of the thinnest portion of the lesion to minimize the resulting scar

exp: A suspected melanoma is best removed by excisional/complete biopsy with narrow (1–3 mm) margins and adequate depth, which prevents sampling error and lets the pathologist assess overall architecture and microstage accurately. The distractors compromise staging: a superficial shave of one area, FNA, or a thin incisional wedge risk transection and under-staging.
hy: Complete/excisional biopsy (1–3 mm margins) for accurate microstaging.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Investigation and Clinical Diagnosis

stem: Why are superficial shave biopsies generally avoided for a thick or palpable pigmented lesion?

options.A: They risk transecting the tumor base and so underestimating the Breslow thickness
options.B: They cannot provide any usable tissue for routine histopathologic examination at all
options.C: They almost always seed and disseminate melanoma cells along the biopsy track
options.D: They are contraindicated mainly because of the substantial bleeding risk they carry

exp: Superficial shave biopsies are avoided for thick/palpable lesions because they risk transecting the tumor base, which underestimates Breslow thickness and corrupts staging (though a broad shave can be useful for thin MIS, LM type). The distractors are false: shave biopsies do yield histology, do not seed melanoma along the track, and the concern is transection, not bleeding.
hy: Avoid superficial shave on palpable lesions → risks transection/under-staging.

stem: A patient has an invasive melanoma 2.5 mm thick (>2–4 mm). What is the recommended surgical (wide local excision) margin?

options.A: 2.0 cm
options.B: 0.5 cm
options.C: 1.0 cm
options.D: 4.0 cm

exp: A melanoma >2–4 mm thick is excised with a 2.0 cm margin — the maximum recommended, since randomized trials showed no benefit beyond 2 cm even for thick tumors. The distractors are the margins for thinner tumors (0.5 cm/1 cm for in situ/≤1 mm) or the obsolete 4 cm wide margin.
hy: >2 mm melanoma → 2 cm margin (the ceiling).

stem: What is the recommended margin for a melanoma in situ (non-LM type), and what is the maximum WLE margin recommended for any thickness?

options.A: 0.5–1.0 cm for in situ; 2.0 cm maximum for thick melanoma (no benefit beyond)
options.B: 1.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 5.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.C: 2.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 4.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.D: 0.5 cm for melanoma in situ; a 1.0 cm maximum margin for the thickest tumors

exp: Melanoma in situ is excised with 0.5–1.0 cm margins, and 2.0 cm is the maximum WLE margin recommended for any thickness — RCTs showed no added benefit beyond 2 cm. The distractors inflate the ceiling (5 cm was the historical pre-trial practice; 4 cm was the comparator that proved unnecessary) or misstate the in-situ margin.
hy: In situ 0.5–1.0 cm; 2 cm is the maximum useful margin.

stem: For melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type on the face, which approach is favored?

options.A: Mohs/staged excision with complete margin assessment; subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm to clear
options.B: A fixed 5 mm peripheral margin, which is reliably sufficient for facial lentigo maligna in all cases
options.C: Cryotherapy applied to the visible lesion as the first-line definitive treatment of choice for the face
options.D: Observation without treatment, on the basis that lentigo maligna essentially never progresses to invasion

exp: Facial MIS of the lentigo maligna type is best managed with Mohs or staged excision providing complete circumferential peripheral and deep margin assessment (CCPDMA), because subclinical spread along adnexa may require ≥1 cm for histologic clearance. The distractors are false: a fixed 5 mm margin often fails on the face, cryotherapy is not first-line definitive therapy, and LM (melanoma in situ) can progress.
hy: Facial MIS-LM → Mohs/staged excision (CCPDMA); subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm.

stem: Topical imiquimod is sometimes used for lentigo maligna. What is its mechanism, and when is it typically used?

options.A: A Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist; used as primary therapy when surgery is unsuitable, or adjunctively
options.B: An alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy cream applied directly to the surgical tumor bed after resection
options.C: A topical BRAF-inhibitor preparation applied to V600E-mutant lentigo maligna to shrink it before surgery
options.D: A first-line replacement for wide local excision in essentially all invasive cutaneous melanomas

exp: Imiquimod is a Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist used for lentigo maligna either as primary therapy (elderly/poor surgical candidates, disfiguring facial sites) or adjuvantly when surgical margins are narrow (≤1 mm) or transected with no clinical residual tumor. The distractors miscast it as alkylating chemotherapy, a BRAF inhibitor, or a replacement for surgery in invasive melanoma.
hy: Imiquimod = TLR-7 agonist; LM primary (non-surgical) or adjuvant.

stem: What is the current evidence-based status of Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) for invasive cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: Evidence is insufficient to recommend MMS for invasive melanoma; its recognized role is mainly MIS (LM/acral)
options.B: MMS is the established, guideline-endorsed standard of care for all invasive cutaneous melanomas at any site
options.C: MMS is formally contraindicated for any melanoma whatsoever, including melanoma in situ of the head and neck
options.D: MMS has demonstrated clearly superior melanoma-specific survival in large prospective randomized trials

exp: Evidence is currently insufficient for guidelines to recommend MMS for *invasive* melanoma; its recognized value is in melanoma in situ — LM/CSD type and acral type (and minimally invasive T1a LMM at constrained sites) — where complete margin assessment improves local control. The distractors overstate (standard for all invasive melanoma; proven MSS benefit) or wrongly forbid it entirely.
hy: MMS: established for MIS (LM/acral), not yet recommended for invasive melanoma.

stem: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is approved for which resected node-negative (stage II) melanomas?

options.A: Stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and stage IIC (T4b N0)
options.B: Stage IA disease (a T1a N0 thin melanoma)
options.C: All resected melanoma in-situ (stage 0) lesions
options.D: Only resected stage IV distant metastatic disease

exp: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is approved for resected node-negative stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and IIC (T4b N0) melanoma, based on a phase 3 trial showing reduced recurrence/death. The distractors are out of indication: stage IA and in situ are too low-risk, and the stage II approval is specifically about resected node-negative high-risk primaries, not stage IV.
hy: Adjuvant pembrolizumab: resected stage IIB/IIC.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Adjuvant Therapy for Stage II

stem: For which clinical scenario is sentinel lymph node biopsy NOT typically recommended?

options.A: T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, no ulceration), with SLN positivity under 5%
options.B: T2a melanoma (>1–2 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)
options.C: T3b melanoma (>2–4 mm thick, with histologic ulceration of the primary)
options.D: T4a melanoma (>4 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)

exp: SLNB is NOT typically recommended for T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm without ulceration), where the probability of a positive node is <5%. The distractors are scenarios where SLNB IS discussed/offered: T2a (>10% positivity), and the higher-risk T3b and T4a tumors.
hy: No routine SLNB for T1a (<0.8 mm, no ulcer; <5% positive).

stem: Approximately what proportion of clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick have a positive sentinel node?
exp: Roughly 15–20% of clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick harbor microscopic nodal disease found on SLNB, with the rate rising with thickness, ulceration, high mitotic index, and lymphovascular invasion. The distractors are far off the cited figure.
hy: ~15–20% SLNB positivity for melanoma >1 mm, node-negative clinically.

stem: During SLNB, which is a criterion for identifying a sentinel node intraoperatively?

options.A: A node emitting ≥10% of the hottest node's tracer counts (and/or dyed blue or palpably enlarged)
options.B: Only the single anatomically largest node in the basin, irrespective of tracer uptake or dye
options.C: Specifically any node that fails to take up either the blue dye or the radiotracer at all
options.D: All lymph nodes lying within 5 cm of the primary tumor, removed empirically en bloc

exp: Sentinel nodes are defined intraoperatively as any palpably enlarged node, any node dyed blue, the node with the highest radiotracer emission, and any node emitting ≥10% of the counts of the hottest node. The distractors contradict this (a single largest node, non-uptaking nodes, or empiric removal of all nearby nodes).
hy: SLN = blue, hottest, ≥10% of hottest, or palpable.

stem: What best describes the role of SLNB in melanoma?

options.A: A prognostic/staging procedure not shown to improve survival, but it finds occult stage III disease
options.B: A therapeutic procedure that, by removing the first draining node, independently cures regional nodal disease
options.C: A procedure that has now replaced wide local excision as the definitive treatment of the primary tumor
options.D: A staging step that is required in every melanoma, including thin melanoma in situ (stage 0) lesions

exp: SLNB is primarily a prognostic/staging procedure: apart from select MSLT-1 subgroups it has not been shown to improve overall survival, but it identifies occult stage III disease, enabling relapse-free-survival–improving adjuvant therapy. The distractors overstate it (curative/therapeutic, replaces WLE) or wrongly extend it to in situ disease.
hy: SLNB = prognostic, not therapeutic; enables adjuvant therapy.

stem: Following a positive SLNB, why has completion lymph node dissection (CLND) declined?

options.A: Two RCTs (MSLT-2, DeCOG) showed no overall-survival benefit versus nodal-ultrasound surveillance
options.B: Completion dissection was actually shown in randomized trials to worsen melanoma-specific survival
options.C: Completion dissection is technically impossible to perform once the sentinel node has been removed
options.D: Nodal ultrasound surveillance now provides full pathologic non-sentinel-node data, making it redundant

exp: CLND after a positive SLNB has declined because two RCTs (MSLT-2 and DeCOG) showed no overall-survival benefit versus observation with nodal-ultrasound surveillance. The distractors are false: CLND did not worsen MSS, it is technically feasible, and ultrasound does NOT provide pathologic non-sentinel-node information (a stated limitation).
hy: No OS benefit for CLND (MSLT-2/DeCOG) → nodal US surveillance.

stem: A patient develops indurated papulonodules within 2 cm of the melanoma excision scar (satellitosis). How does this change staging?

options.A: It reclassifies the disease as stage III — prompting imaging, wide excision/possible SLNB, and adjuvant discussion
options.B: It remains stage I provided the original primary tumor was thin and had been completely excised
options.C: It is automatically stage IV distant metastatic disease regardless of how close it lies to the scar
options.D: It carries no staging implication at all as long as the original surgical margins were histologically clear

exp: Satellitosis (papulonodules within ~2 cm of the scar) is lymphatic (in-transit) disease that reclassifies the melanoma as stage III, prompting staging imaging, referral for wide excision and possible SLNB, and discussion of adjuvant systemic therapy. The distractors understage it (remaining stage I, no implication) or overstage it (automatically stage IV distant disease).
hy: Satellite/in-transit recurrence = stage III → restage + adjuvant discussion.

stem: Talimogene laherparepvec (TVEC), used intralesionally for unresectable cutaneous/in-transit metastases, works by:

options.A: Being an engineered herpes virus that replicates in tumor cells and secretes GM-CSF to drive antitumor immunity
options.B: Acting as a systemically administered alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy that crosslinks tumor-cell DNA
options.C: Combining a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor to shut down constitutive MAPK pathway signaling
options.D: Functioning as an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that releases the brake on cytotoxic T lymphocytes

exp: TVEC is a genetically engineered herpes simplex virus that selectively replicates in tumor cells, lysing them and producing GM-CSF to recruit and stimulate an antitumor immune response — used intralesionally for unresectable cutaneous/in-transit disease. The distractors describe entirely different modalities (alkylating chemotherapy, BRAF/MEK inhibition, anti-PD-1 antibody).
hy: TVEC = oncolytic engineered HSV expressing GM-CSF.
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

stem: For multiple in-transit metastases confined to one limb, which regional treatment may have palliative (sometimes curative) value?

options.A: Isolated limb perfusion using melphalan, with or without added TNF
options.B: Whole-brain external-beam radiotherapy delivered over several fractions
options.C: Systemic single-agent dacarbazine chemotherapy given intravenously
options.D: Prophylactic completion lymphadenectomy of the regional nodal basin

exp: For multiple in-transit metastases confined to a limb, isolated limb perfusion with melphalan (± TNF) has palliative and sometimes curative value (reported 5-/10-year survival ~40%/30%). The distractors are inappropriate here: whole-brain RT is for brain disease, single-agent dacarbazine is weak salvage chemotherapy, and prophylactic lymphadenectomy does not treat in-transit disease.
hy: Limb in-transit disease → isolated limb perfusion (melphalan ± TNF).
src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

stem: For resected stage III melanoma with a BRAF V600 mutation, which adjuvant targeted regimen is approved?

options.A: Dabrafenib plus trametinib (a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor)
options.B: Imatinib monotherapy directed against the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.C: High-dose interleukin-2 immunostimulation given as inpatient therapy
options.D: Larotrectinib, the selective TRK inhibitor used for NTRK-fusion tumors

exp: For resected stage III BRAF V600-mutant melanoma, adjuvant dabrafenib plus trametinib (BRAF + MEK inhibitors) is approved. The distractors target the wrong lesion or era: imatinib is for KIT-mutant disease, high-dose IL-2 is older unresectable-disease therapy, and larotrectinib is for NTRK-fusion tumors.
hy: Adjuvant dabrafenib+trametinib for resected stage III BRAF V600+.

stem: Why are BRAF inhibitors combined with MEK inhibitors in BRAF-mutant melanoma?

options.A: The combination delays resistance (response ~10 months) and cuts the cutaneous SCCs/KAs of BRAFi monotherapy
options.B: The MEK inhibitor is required to activate the BRAF inhibitor, which is an inactive prodrug on its own
options.C: Adding a MEK inhibitor reliably eliminates all of the immune-related adverse events of the regimen
options.D: The MEK inhibitor is needed to treat the NRAS mutation that every BRAF-mutant melanoma also carries

exp: A MEK inhibitor is added to a BRAF inhibitor because the combination delays acquired resistance (extending average response to ~10 months) and reduces the keratinocyte side effects (cutaneous SCCs, keratoacanthomas) caused by BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy. The distractors are false mechanisms: BRAF inhibitors are not prodrugs needing MEK, the combination does not eliminate irAEs, and not all BRAF-mutant tumors carry NRAS mutations.
hy: BRAFi+MEKi: delays resistance (~10 mo) and cuts cuSCC/KA.

stem: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy induces cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas/keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients because of:

options.A: Paradoxical MAPK pathway activation in wild-type-BRAF (often RAS-mutant) keratinocytes
options.B: Direct chemical mutagenesis of keratinocyte DNA by the small-molecule drug itself
options.C: Drug-induced local immunosuppression of the skin permitting keratinocyte outgrowth
options.D: Reactivation of latent oncogenic human papillomavirus within the epidermal keratinocytes

exp: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy causes cutaneous SCCs/keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients through *paradoxical* MAPK activation in keratinocytes that have wild-type BRAF and frequently RAS mutations — which is why these lesions fall when a MEK inhibitor is added. The distractors propose mechanisms the chapter does not support (direct DNA mutagenesis, immunosuppression, HPV reactivation).
hy: BRAFi → paradoxical MAPK in RAS-mutant keratinocytes → cuSCC/KA (~25%).

stem: Which statement about first-line systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic melanoma is correct?

options.A: ICIs are generally preferred first-line regardless of BRAF status; ipilimumab is largely supplanted by anti-PD-1 agents
options.B: BRAF inhibitors are recommended first-line even for tumors that are confirmed to be BRAF-wild-type on testing
options.C: Single-agent ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) is preferred over anti-PD-1 monotherapy because it is less toxic overall
options.D: Cytotoxic chemotherapy with dacarbazine remains the standard first-line treatment of metastatic melanoma

exp: Immune checkpoint inhibitors are generally preferred first-line for unresectable/metastatic melanoma regardless of BRAF status (durable responses), and the original anti-CTLA-4 agent ipilimumab has been largely supplanted by the more effective, less toxic anti-PD-1 antibodies nivolumab and pembrolizumab. The distractors are false: BRAF inhibitors require a BRAF mutation, single-agent ipilimumab is inferior to anti-PD-1, and chemotherapy is now salvage-only.
hy: ICIs first-line (any BRAF status); anti-PD-1 > ipilimumab.

stem: Approximately what proportion of cutaneous melanomas carry an activating BRAF mutation, and what fraction of BRAF mutations are V600E?

options.A: ~45% are BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E
options.B: ~80% are BRAF-mutant; about half of those mutations are V600E
options.C: ~15% are BRAF-mutant; only about 10% of those are V600E
options.D: ~95% are BRAF-mutant; nearly all of those are V600K, not V600E

exp: About 45% of cutaneous melanomas carry an activating BRAF mutation (and ~15% NRAS), and roughly 90% of those BRAF mutations are V600E (glutamic acid for valine). The distractors use wrong proportions (80%/50%; 15%/10%; 95% all-V600K).
hy: ~45% BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E.

stem: Why is caution needed when sequencing a BRAF inhibitor shortly after an immune checkpoint inhibitor?

options.A: Severe drug hypersensitivity (DRESS) occurs in ~20% when a BRAFi follows ICI priming, versus ~5% without
options.B: The overlapping combination predictably triggers an immediate, life-threatening tumor lysis syndrome
options.C: Immune checkpoint inhibitors permanently and irreversibly inactivate the BRAF inhibitor molecule
options.D: The sequence reliably cures essentially all treated patients, so no special caution is actually needed

exp: Severe drug hypersensitivity, including DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms), occurs in ~20% of patients when a BRAF inhibitor is given soon after immune checkpoint inhibitor "priming," versus ~5% without prior ICI — a reason to be cautious with sequencing. The distractors invent unrelated mechanisms (tumor lysis, BRAFi inactivation) or wrongly claim the sequence is uniformly safe/curative.
hy: BRAFi soon after ICI → ~20% DRESS (vs ~5%).

stem: Which is an appropriate use of radiation therapy in melanoma?

options.A: Stereotactic radiosurgery for brain mets; primary RT for unresectable MIS-LM; adjuvant RT for high-risk desmoplastic tumors
options.B: Routine first-line definitive treatment of all localized cutaneous melanomas in place of surgical excision
options.C: Routine adjuvant irradiation of the nodal basin after lymphadenectomy, on the basis of a proven survival benefit
options.D: As a standard substitute for wide local excision in the management of thin (T1) cutaneous melanomas

exp: Radiation has defined niches in melanoma: stereotactic radiosurgery for brain metastases, primary RT for unresectable MIS (LM type), and adjuvant RT for high-risk (e.g. neurotropic) desmoplastic melanoma. The distractors are wrong: RT is rarely used for localized cutaneous melanoma, routine adjuvant nodal-basin RT showed no survival benefit (only local control at cost of toxicity), and RT does not replace wide excision of thin melanomas.
hy: RT niches: SRS for brain mets, unresectable MIS-LM, adjuvant for desmoplastic.

stem: Which statement about melanoma recurrence and second primaries is correct?

options.A: About 90% of recurrences occur within 5 years (greatest in the first 2); second-primary risk is ~3.5–4.5%
options.B: Recurrences are spread evenly across the first 20 years of follow-up, showing no early post-diagnosis peak
options.C: Late recurrence occurring beyond 10–15 years from diagnosis is by far the most common pattern of relapse
options.D: The lifetime risk of developing a second primary cutaneous melanoma exceeds 50% in most treated patients

exp: About 90% of melanoma recurrences occur within the first 5 years (greatest in the first 2), and the lifetime risk of a second primary melanoma is ~3.5–4.5% — hence lifelong surveillance. The distractors are false: recurrences are front-loaded (not evenly spread), late recurrence beyond 10–15 years is uncommon (not the dominant pattern), and second-primary risk is single-digit, not >50%.
hy: 90% of recurrences in first 5 yr; second-primary risk ~3.5–4.5%.

stem: A typical recommended dermatologic follow-up schedule after melanoma diagnosis is:

options.A: 1–4 times/year for 2 years, then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually
options.B: A single comprehensive skin examination visit at the 5-year mark, followed by discharge from care
options.C: Weekly dermatologic visits continued indefinitely for the remainder of the patient's lifetime
options.D: Whole-body imaging every month for life, regardless of the original stage at diagnosis

exp: Most experts recommend dermatologic visits 1–4 times per year for the first 2 years (risk-adjusted), then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually thereafter — matching the front-loaded recurrence risk. The distractors are implausible extremes (single 5-year visit; weekly visits; monthly lifelong imaging).
hy: Follow-up: 1–4×/yr ×2 yr → q6–12 mo to year 5 → annually.
